title: Add Frequencies for Selected Categories
description: Add the counts (number of Xs) for specified values on a line plot to find a combined total.
condition: Given a line plot and a question asking for the total number of data points in two or more specified categories (e.g., 1, 2, or 3 pets).
response: Add the frequencies (number of Xs) above each specified value to find the total.
label: Principle
example: If there are 5 Xs above 1, 4 Xs above 2, and 1 X above 3, then total = $5 + 4 + 1 = 10$.
notes: Requires interpreting each X as one data point.

title: Add Three Two-Digit Numbers
description: Add three two-digit numbers to find a total sum.
condition: Given three two-digit numbers to combine (e.g., total distance jumped).
response: Compute the total by adding all three numbers accurately.
label: Principle
notes: May involve place-value strategies or standard algorithm.

title: Align Ruler at Zero
description: Line up the 0 mark of a ruler with one endpoint of an object before measuring.
condition: Given a physical object and a ruler with a marked 0 point.
response: Place the 0 mark at one endpoint of the object before reading the measurement.
label: Production / Skill
example: To measure a rectangle, place the 0 cm mark at the left end before reading the right end.
notes: Foundational measuring behavior.

title: Count Centimeter Units
description: Count the number of centimeter units that span the length of an object.
condition: An object is aligned with a centimeter ruler.
response: Count the centimeter marks from 0 to the endpoint to determine length.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the object ends at the 12 mark, count 12 centimeter units.
notes: Requires accurate one‑to‑one correspondence.

title: Count Total Data Points on Line Plot
description: Determine the total number of data points represented on a line plot by counting all X marks.
condition: Given a complete line plot with Xs representing data points and asked for the total number surveyed.
response: Count all X marks on the plot to find the total number of data points.
label: Production / Skill
example: If there are 17 Xs shown on the plot, then 17 students took the survey.
notes: May be done by counting individually or by adding grouped counts.

title: Decompose for Subtraction Within 100
description: Break apart numbers by tens and ones to subtract within 100.
condition: A two-digit subtraction problem within 100 (e.g., 37 − 13).
response: Subtract tens and ones separately or decompose one number (e.g., 37 − 10 − 3).
label: Principle / Model
notes: Based on place value structure.

title: Express Measurement with Unit Label
description: State a measured length using the correct unit (inches).
condition: A length has been measured in inch units.
response: Report the numerical value followed by the unit 'inches'.
label: Rule / Plan
example: The rectangle measures $5$ inches.
notes: Emphasizes inclusion of appropriate measurement unit.

title: Identify Reasonable Height Estimate with Units
description: Select the measurement with an appropriate unit and reasonable magnitude for a person's height.
condition: Given several numerical height estimates with different units for a second grader.
response: Choose the estimate that matches typical human height (e.g., 50 inches).
label: Concept
example: Among 50 cm, 50 in, 50 ft, 50 m, select 50 inches as reasonable.
notes: Relies on real-world knowledge of typical human heights.

title: Interpret "Longer Than" as Difference
description: Translate comparison language such as '13 cm longer than' into an addition or subtraction relationship.
condition: A comparison statement of the form 'A is k units longer than B.'
response: Represent the relationship as A = B + k or B = A − k.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If Noah is 13 cm longer than Tyler and Noah is 37 cm, then $T = 37 - 13$.
notes: Language-to-equation translation skill.

title: Interpret Decrease as Subtraction
description: Recognize that language indicating removal means quantities should be subtracted.
condition: A word problem states an amount was 'cut off', 'trimmed', 'removed', or 'less'.
response: Represent the situation using subtraction.
label: Rule / Plan
example: "Cut off 15 inches" → compute $42 - 15$.
notes: Relies on conventional problem-language cues.

title: Interpret Each X as One Data Value
description: Understand that each X on a line plot represents one instance of the measured quantity.
condition: Given a line plot where X marks are placed above numbers on a number line.
response: Interpret each X as representing one data point with that value.
label: Concept
example: An X above 2 on the line plot means one student has 2 pets.
notes: Conventional representation of line plots.

title: Interpret Growth as Addition
description: Recognize that language indicating an increase means quantities should be added.
condition: A word problem states an amount 'grew', 'increased', or 'more' than an initial amount.
response: Represent the situation using addition.
label: Rule / Plan
example: "It grew 34 inches" → compute $8 + 34$.
notes: Conventional interpretation of comparison/change language.

title: Label Line Plot Scale Including Range
description: Determine and label an appropriate numeric scale on a line plot that includes the minimum and maximum data values.
condition: Given a set of data values and a blank number line for a line plot.
response: Write consecutive numbers spanning at least from the smallest to the largest data value.
label: Principle
example: For data 13 to 25, label the line plot from 12 to 26 to include all values.
notes: Scale choice may vary slightly but must include full data range.

title: Label Line Plot with Title and Axis
description: Provide an appropriate title and label for the measurement axis of a line plot.
condition: Given data to display on a line plot and instructed to include a title and axis label.
response: Write a descriptive title and label the axis with the measured quantity and units.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Title: "Seedling Heights"; Axis label: "height in centimeters."
notes: Follows conventional structure of graphs.

title: Match X Position to Numerical Value
description: Determine the data value represented by the position of an X on the number line.
condition: Given an X placed above a tick mark on a labeled number line.
response: Identify the number on the axis directly below the X as the data value.
label: Production / Skill
example: If an X is above 4 on the number line, the data value is 4.
notes: Requires understanding of number line alignment.

title: Measure Length in Centimeters
description: Measure an object's length using a centimeter ruler.
condition: Given a physical rectangle and a centimeter ruler.
response: Align the ruler at zero and read the length in centimeters.
label: Production / Skill
example: Rectangle A measures 16 cm when aligned to the ruler's zero mark.
notes: Procedural measurement skill.

title: Measure Length with an Inch Ruler
description: Determine an object's length by aligning a ruler at zero and counting inch units to the endpoint.
condition: Given a physical object and an inch ruler with marked units.
response: Align the 0 mark with one end and count the number of whole inches to the other end.
label: Production / Skill
example: Align the ruler at 0 and count to the endpoint: 5 inch marks → $5$ inches.
notes: Includes correct alignment at zero and counting unit intervals.

title: Place One X per Data Value
description: Represent each occurrence of a data value by placing one X above the corresponding number on the number line.
condition: Given a list of numerical data values to represent on a line plot.
response: For each data value, place one X above the matching number on the number line, stacking Xs for repeated values.
label: Production / Skill
example: Given data $3,2,1,4,2,3,2,1,1,2$, place three Xs above 1, four Xs above 2, two Xs above 3, and one X above 4.
notes: All Xs should be the same size and aligned vertically.

title: Represent Comparison with Bar Model
description: Draw a diagram showing one quantity as longer than another by a specified difference.
condition: A comparison story problem with a known total and known difference.
response: Draw two aligned bars showing the total and the extra part representing the difference.
label: Production / Skill
example: Draw Noah’s bar labeled 37 cm and Tyler’s shorter bar with an extra 13 cm segment shown.
notes: Diagram must match quantitative relationships.

title: Represent Data on Line Plot
description: Plot each data value as a mark above the corresponding number on a labeled line plot.
condition: Given labeled line plot and a list of numerical data values.
response: Place one mark (e.g., X) above each value for every occurrence in the data set.
label: Production / Skill
example: For data 20, 20, 20, place three Xs above 20.
notes: Includes counting frequency of repeated values.

title: Smaller Unit Produces Larger Count
description: For the same object, a smaller measurement unit results in a larger numerical measurement.
condition: Comparing measurements of the same object expressed in two different-sized units (e.g., centimeters and inches).
response: Assign the larger number to the smaller unit and explain that smaller units require more to measure the same length.
label: Principle
example: If a pencil measures 6 and 15, then 15 is centimeters because centimeters are smaller than inches.
notes: Relies on understanding relative unit sizes.

title: Subtract to Compare Lengths
description: Find how much longer one length is than another by subtracting the smaller from the larger.
condition: Given two measured lengths and asked how much longer one is.
response: Compute larger minus smaller to find the difference.
label: Principle
example: If A = 16 cm and B = 9 cm, compute $16 - 9 = 7$ cm.
notes: Requires identifying which length is greater first.
